revision.comment: Sarutahiko, Shinto diety of the earth
revision.text: Sarutahiko (Sarutahiko-no-Oh-Kami) Is A Kami (Diety) of Shinto, the indiginous religion of Japan. Sarutahiko is many times credited as being the guardian of the earth, and appeared in the Kojiki as the Kami who guided the Sun goddess Amaterasu's grandchild from heaven to earth. He is the Main Diety of Tsubaki Jinja, one of the Oldest shines in Japan celebrating its 2000 year anaversary in 1997.

He is the Main Diety of Tsubaki Jinja, one of the Oldest shines in Japan celebrating its 2000 year anaversary in 1997.

'''Sarutahiko-no-Oh-Kami''' is a [[kami]], a deity of the [[Japan|Japanese]] religion of [[Shinto]].
Sarutahiko is a powerful guradian kami whom is enshrined in the name of Tsubaki Jinja and the Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America. he is the leader of earthy kami and guarding of the floating bridge of heaven. He appears in the Kojiki (recored of ancient matters) number of times along with his wife Ame-No-Uzume. sarutahiko is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, and distinctive nose. Sarutahiko is also a figur of strength and willpower. This is depicted in a section of Kojiki that describes the Grandson of the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu who is ready to take control of the world. He refuses to leave earth untill his soon-to-be wife, Ame-No-Uzume comes provocitively to convincehim to change his mind. Today, most american shintoists are devout followers in the teachings of Sarutahiko for many are associated with the Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America. [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[ja:サルタヒコ]] [[pl:Sarutahiko]]

revision.comment: Rewriting to correct the English and a factual error about American Shintoists
Links words to relevant topics, corrected and extended kami names, changed katakana to proper kanji for Sarutahiko Ohkami
Added etymological analysis of Sarutahiko's name
revision.text:
'''Sarutahiko Ohkami''' (猿田毘古神), is a [[kami]], a deity of the [[Japan|Japanese]] religion of [[Shinto]].
Sarutahiko Ohkami is a powerful guardian kami who is enshrined at Tsubaki Jinja in Mie Prefecture and at Sarutahiko Jinja in Ise. In the [[Kojiki]], he is the leader of the earthly kami and the one who greets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara]]. He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to yield his realm until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume]] no Mikoto, the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries. Sarutahiko Ohkami is seen as a symbol of strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]].
His name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is transcribed with Chinese characters (猿田) that suggest the meaning "monkey-field," followed by the Classical Japanese noun ''hiko'' "a male child of royal blood, a prince."
{{Japan-myth-stub}} {{religion-stub}}
{{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]
[[fr:Saruta-hiko]] [[ja:サルタヒコ]] [[pl:Sarutahiko]]

His name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is transcribed with Chinese characters (猿田) that suggest the meaning "monkey-field," followed by the Classical Japanese noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."

revision.comment: Added paragraph about Sarutahiko being the only "kunitsukami" honored with the title of "ookami"
revision.text:
'''Sarutahiko Ohkami''' (猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神), is a [[kami]], a deity of the [[Japan|Japanese]] religion of [[Shinto]].
Sarutahiko Ohkami is a powerful guardian kami who is enshrined at Tsubaki Jinja in Mie Prefecture and at Sarutahiko Jinja in Ise. In the [[Kojiki]], he is the leader of the earthly kami and the one who greets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara]]. He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to yield his realm until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume]] no Mikoto, the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries. Sarutahiko Ohkami is seen as a symbol of strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]].
His name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with Chinese characters (猿田) that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the Classical Japanese noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince." Thus, Sarutahiko Ohkami's name could be roughly translated into English as "Great Kami, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ohkami'' or ''Mikoto''.
Sarutahiko has the distinction of being one of only six kami to be honored with the title ''Ohkami'' (大神) or "Great Kami"; the other five are [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] (in her [[Persephone]]-like role as Queen of the Underworld), [[Michikaeshi]], [[Sashikuni]], and [[Amaterasu]]. The special honor paid to Sarutahiko is particularly notable for the fact that he is the singular ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, to be given the title; the other five are all ''amatsukami'', i.e. heavenly kami.
{{Japan-myth-stub}} {{religion-stub}}
{{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]
[[fr:Saruta-hiko]] [[ja:サルタヒコ]] [[pl:Sarutahiko]]

His name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with Chinese characters (猿田) that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the Classical Japanese noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince." Sarutahiko has the distinction of being one of only six kami to be honored with the title ''Ohkami'' (大神) or "Great Kami"; the other five are [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] (in her [[Persephone]]-like role as Queen of the Underworld), [[Michikaeshi]], [[Sashikuni]], and [[Amaterasu]]. '''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' (猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神), is a [[kami]], a deity of the [[Japan|Japanese]] religion of [[Shinto]].

Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]].

Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.
Sarutahiko has the distinction of being one of only six kami to be honored with the title ''Ōkami'' (大神) or "Great Kami"; the other five are [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] (in her [[Persephone]]-like role as Queen of the Underworld), [[Michikaeshi]], [[Sashikuni]], and [[Amaterasu]]. '''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' (猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神), is a ''[[kami]]'', a deity of the [[Japan|Japanese]] religion of [[Shinto]].
Sarutahiko Ōkami is a powerful guardian ''kami'' who is enshrined at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine|Tsubaki Jinja]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], Sarutahiko Jinja in [[Ise, Mie|Ise]] and [[Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[Tokushima Prefecture]]. In the [[Kojiki]], he is the leader of the earthly ''kami'' and the one who greets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara]]. He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to yield his realm until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the ''kami'' of dance and the arts, whom he later marries. Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]].
His name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[kanji]] (猿田) that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the Classical Japanese noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince." Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great Kami, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.
Sarutahiko has the distinction of being one of only six ''kami'' to be honored with the title ''Ōkami'' (大神) or "Great Kami"; the other five are [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] (in her [[Persephone]]-like role as Queen of the Underworld), [[Michikaeshi]], [[Sashikuni]], and [[Amaterasu]]. The special honor paid to Sarutahiko is particularly notable for the fact that he is the singular ''kunitsukami'', or earthly ''kami'', to be given the title; the other five are all ''amatsukami'', i.e. heavenly ''kami''.

[[fr:Saruta-hiko]] [[ja:サルタヒコ]] [[pl:Sarutahiko]]

'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' (猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神), is a ''[[kami]]'', a deity of the [[Japan|Japanese]] religion of [[Shinto]].
Sarutahiko Ōkami is a powerful guardian ''kami'' who is enshrined at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], Sarutahiko Jinja in [[Ise, Mie|Ise]] and [[Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[Tokushima Prefecture]]. In the [[Kojiki]], he is the leader of the earthly ''kami'' and the one who greets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara]]. He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to yield his realm until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the ''kami'' of dance and the arts, whom he later marries. Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]].
His name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[kanji]] (猿田) that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the Classical Japanese noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince." Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great Kami, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.
Sarutahiko has the distinction of being one of only six ''kami'' to be honored with the title ''Ōkami'' (大神) or "Great Kami"; the other five are [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] (in her [[Persephone]]-like role as Queen of the Underworld), [[Michikaeshi]], [[Sashikuni]], and [[Amaterasu]]. The special honor paid to Sarutahiko is particularly notable for the fact that he is the singular ''kunitsukami'', or earthly ''kami'', to be given the title; the other five are all ''amatsukami'', i.e. heavenly ''kami''.
{{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}
{{jmyth navbox long}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]
[[fr:Saruta-hiko]] [[ja:サルタヒコ]] [[pl:Sarutahiko]]

moved [[Sarutahilo]] to [[Sarutahiko Okami]]: Moving revisions as part of [[Wikipedia:WikiProject History Merge|history merge]]
revision.text: Sarutahiko (Sarutahiko-no-Oh-Kami) Is A Kami (Diety) of Shinto, the indiginous religion of Japan. Sarutahiko is many times credited as being the guardian of the earth, and appeared in the Kojiki as the Kami who guided the Sun goddess Amaterasu's grandchild from heaven to earth. He is the Main Diety of Tsubaki Jinja, one of the Oldest shines in Japan celebrating its 2000 year anaversary in 1997.

{{unreferenced|date=March 2010}} {{Shinto}}
'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' (猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神), is a ''[[kami]]'', a deity of the [[Japan]]ese religion of [[Shinto]].
Sarutahiko Ōkami is a powerful guardian ''kami'' who is enshrined at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], first among the 2,000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami and Sarutahiko Jinja in [[Ise, Mie|Ise]] and [[Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[Tokushima Prefecture]]. In the [[Kojiki]], he is the leader of the earthly ''kami'' and the one who greets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara]]. He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to yield his realm until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the ''kami'' of dance and the arts, whom he later marries. Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]].
[[File:Sarutahiko_Ōkami.crop.4.wp.working.print.png|thumb|left|160px|Sarutahiko Ōkami, in a somewhat comical depiction; taken from a late-19th century Japanese painting.]]
His name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[kanji]] (猿田) that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the Classical Japanese noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince." Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.
Sarutahiko has the distinction of being one of only six ''kami'' to be honored with the title ''Ōkami'' (大神) or "Great Kami"; the other five are [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] (in her [[Persephone]]-like role as Queen of the Underworld), [[Michikaeshi]], [[Sashikuni]], and [[Amaterasu]]. The special honor paid to Sarutahiko is particularly notable for the fact that he is the singular ''kunitsukami'', or earthly ''kami'', to be given the title; the other five are all ''amatsukami'', i.e., heavenly ''kami''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2009}}
===References in music=== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.

revision.text:
{{Shinto}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}} '''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' (猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神), is the leader of the earthly [[kami]], deity of the [[ethnic religion|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title ''Daimyōjin'' (大明神 great bright god, or greatly virtuous god) instead of Ōkami (大神 great god).
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and [[guidance]]{{dn|date=January 2020}}, which is why he is the patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18&lpg=PA18}}</ref> He enshrined at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in [[Ise, Mie]] and [[Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[Tokushima Prefecture]]. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who greets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|accessdate=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to yield his realm until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|accessdate=13 August 2012}}</ref>
His Paredros and children created the ''Sarume no Kimi'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on Isuzu river at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life : the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself (and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself)... which doesn't fit very well this Kojiki story of Sarutahiko's death (although it is unknown if he really died from it).
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami.crop.4.wp.working.print.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[kanji]] that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist [[Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney]] lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== Sarutahiko has the distinction of being one of only seven [[kami]] to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami]], [[Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), [[Sashikuni]], [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]], and [[Amaterasu]]. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''Amatsukami'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared latter in the History of Japanese Religion, such as Hachiman (deification of Empress Jingu, Emperor Ojin and maybe Takeuchi no Sukune, as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. {{Citation needed|date=August 2009}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|accessdate=13 August 2012}}</ref>

{{Shinto}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}} '''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' (猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神), is the leader of the earthly [[kami]], deity of the [[ethnic religion|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title ''Daimyōjin'' (大明神 great bright god, or greatly virtuous god) instead of Ōkami (大神 great god).
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and [[wikt:Special:Search/guidance|guidance]], which is why he is the patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18&lpg=PA18}}</ref> He enshrined at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in [[Ise, Mie]] and [[Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[Tokushima Prefecture]]. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who greets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|accessdate=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to yield his realm until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|accessdate=13 August 2012}}</ref>
His Paredros and children created the ''Sarume no Kimi'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on Isuzu river at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life : the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself (and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself)... which doesn't fit very well this Kojiki story of Sarutahiko's death (although it is unknown if he really died from it).

Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}

According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself (and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself)... which doesn't fit very well this Kojiki story of Sarutahiko's death (although it is unknown if he really died from it).
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami.crop.4.wp.working.print.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[kanji]] that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist [[Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney]] lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== Sarutahiko has the distinction of being one of only seven [[kami]] to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami]], [[Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), [[Sashikuni]], [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]], and [[Amaterasu]]. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''[[Amatsukami]]'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared latter in the History of Japanese Religion, such as Hachiman (deification of Empress Jingu, Emperor Ojin and maybe Takeuchi no Sukune, as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. {{Citation needed|date=August 2009}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Tengu]] ** [[Garuda]] * [[Shirahige Jinja]] * [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]]

{{Shinto}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}} '''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' (猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神), is the leader of the earthly [[kami]], deity of the [[ethnic religion|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title ''Daimyōjin'' (大明神 great bright god, or greatly virtuous god) instead of Ōkami (大神 great god).
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and [[wikt:Special:Search/guidance|guidance]], which is why he is the patron of martial arts such as [[aikido]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18&lpg=PA18}}</ref> He enshrined at [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] in [[Mie Prefecture]], first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in [[Ise, Mie]] and [[Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[Tokushima Prefecture]]. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&q=Ninigi&hl=en|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>
His Paredros and children created the ''Sarume no Kimi'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on Isuzu river at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life : the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself (and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself)... which doesn't fit very well this Kojiki story of Sarutahiko's death (although it is unknown if he really died from it).
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami.crop.4.wp.working.print.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[kanji]] that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist [[Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney]] lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== Sarutahiko has the distinction of being one of only seven [[kami]] to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami-no-Mikoto|Izanami]], [[Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), [[Sashikuni]], [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]], and [[Amaterasu]]. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''[[Amatsukami]]'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as Hachiman (deification of Empress Jingu, Emperor Ojin and maybe Takeuchi no Sukune, as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. {{Citation needed|date=August 2009}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>
==See also== * [[Tengu]] ** [[Garuda]] * [[Shirahige Jinja]] * [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|qid=Q10940674}}; {{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|qid=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|qid=Q10949054}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3090037]] Wikidata name: Sarutahiko Ōkami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Deity in Shinto; leader of the earthly kami}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}}
'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[:en:Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/ |access-date=2023-08-21 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of [[:en:Japanese martial arts|martial arts]] such as [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine|Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]]; and [[:en:Ōasahiko Shrine|Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]], and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers.<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[:en:Noh|Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on [[Isuzu River|Isuzu river]] at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[:en:Ueshiba Morihei|Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life: the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[:en:Aikidō|Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself ("and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself"){{cn|date=May 2023}}
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[:en:Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist [[:en:Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney]] lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]]<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[az:Sarutahiko]] {{Commons category|Sarutahiko Ōkami}} [[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]] [[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]] [[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]] [[ja:サルタヒコ]] [[ko:사루타히코 오카미]] [[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]] [[pl:Sarutahiko]] [[ru:Сарутахико]] [[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]] [[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]] [[zh:猿田彥]] {{wikidata link|Q3090037}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[sn:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|qid=Q10940674}}; {{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|qid=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|qid=Q10949054}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3090037]] Wikidata name: Sarutahiko Ōkami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Deity in Shinto; leader of the earthly kami}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}}
'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[:en:Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/ |access-date=2023-08-21 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of [[:en:Japanese martial arts|martial arts]] such as [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine|Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]]; and [[:en:Ōasahiko Shrine|Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]], and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers.<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[:en:Noh|Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on [[Isuzu River|Isuzu river]] at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[:en:Ueshiba Morihei|Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life: the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[:en:Aikidō|Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself ("and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself"){{cn|date=May 2023}}
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[:en:Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist [[:en:Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney]] lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>

Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist [[:en:Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney]] lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]]<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[az:Sarutahiko]] {{Commons category|Sarutahiko Ōkami}} [[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]] [[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]] [[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]] [[ja:サルタヒコ]] [[ko:사루타히코 오카미]] [[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]] [[pl:Sarutahiko]] [[ru:Сарутахико]] [[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]] [[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]] [[zh:猿田彥]] {{wikidata link|Q3090037}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[sn:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|WD=Q10940674}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|WD=Q10949054}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3090037]] Wikidata name: Sarutahiko Ōkami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Deity in Shinto; leader of the earthly kami}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}}
'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[:en:Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/ |access-date=2023-08-21 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of [[:en:Japanese martial arts|martial arts]] such as [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine|Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]]; and [[:en:Ōasahiko Shrine|Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]], and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers.<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[:en:Noh|Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on [[Isuzu River|Isuzu river]] at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[:en:Ueshiba Morihei|Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life: the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[:en:Aikidō|Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself ("and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself"){{cn|date=May 2023}}
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[:en:Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist [[:en:Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney]] lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>

Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist [[:en:Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney]] lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>

<!--Sarutahiko Ōkami-->
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]]<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[az:Sarutahiko]] {{Commons category|Sarutahiko Ōkami}} [[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]] [[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]] [[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]] [[ja:サルタヒコ]] [[ko:사루타히코 오카미]] [[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]] [[pl:Sarutahiko]] [[ru:Сарутахико]] [[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]] [[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]] [[zh:猿田彥]] {{wikidata link|Q3090037}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[sn:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|WD=Q10940674}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|WD=Q10949054}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3090037]] Wikidata name: Sarutahiko Ōkami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Deity in Shinto; leader of the earthly kami}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}}
'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[:en:Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/ |access-date=2023-08-21 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of [[:en:Japanese martial arts|martial arts]] such as [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at [[:en:Tsubaki Grand Shrine|Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]]; and [[:en:Ōasahiko Shrine|Ōasahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]], and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers.<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[:en:Noh|Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on [[Isuzu River|Isuzu river]] at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[:en:Ueshiba Morihei|Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life: the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[:en:Aikidō|Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself ("and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself"){{cn|date=May 2023}}
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[:en:Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist [[:en:Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney]] lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>
