<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[co:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[co:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[sn:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|WD=Q10940674}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|WD=Q10949054}}}} {{Short description|Deity in Shinto; leader of the earthly kami}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}}
'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religion}} of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/ |access-date=2023-08-21 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of {{ill|Japanese martial arts|en|Japanese martial arts|WD=Q1280410|lt=martial arts}} such as {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido}}.<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie}}; and {{ill|Ōasahiko Shrine|en|Ōasahiko Shrine|WD=Q723417|lt=Ōasahiko Shrine}} in {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers.<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}}. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on [[Isuzu River|Isuzu river]] at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[:en:Ueshiba Morihei|Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life: the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[:en:Aikidō|Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself ("and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself"){{cn|date=May 2023}}
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}} that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[:en:Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist {{ill|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|en|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|WD=Q932653|lt=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney}} lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}} ** {{ill|Garuda|en|Garuda|WD=Q188676|lt=Garuda}} * [[Shirahige Shrine|Shirahige Jinja]] * {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}} * [[Sarutahiko Shrine]]
==References== {{Reflist}} {{Sarutahiko Faith}}

'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religion|1=Religion in Japan}} of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of {{ill|Japanese martial arts|en|Japanese martial arts|WD=Q1280410|lt=martial arts|1=Japanese martial arts}} such as {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}.<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}; and {{ill|Ōasahiko Shrine|en|Ōasahiko Shrine|WD=Q723417|lt=Ōasahiko Shrine|1=Ōasahiko Shrine}} in {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].

B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}. [[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]

Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist {{ill|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|en|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|WD=Q932653|lt=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|1=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney}} lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[co:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[co:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|WD=Q10940674|1=Ame no Wakahiko}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696|1=Honoakari}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395|1=Hosuseri}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|WD=Q10949054|1=Nigihayahi}}}} {{Short description|Deity in Shinto; leader of the earthly kami}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}}
'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religion|1=Religion in Japan}} of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/ |access-date=2023-08-21 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of {{ill|Japanese martial arts|en|Japanese martial arts|WD=Q1280410|lt=martial arts|1=Japanese martial arts}} such as {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}.<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}; and {{ill|Ōasahiko Shrine|en|Ōasahiko Shrine|WD=Q723417|lt=Ōasahiko Shrine|1=Ōasahiko Shrine}} in {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers.<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on [[Isuzu River|Isuzu river]] at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[:en:Ueshiba Morihei|Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life: the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[:en:Aikidō|Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself ("and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself"){{cn|date=May 2023}}
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[:en:Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist {{ill|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|en|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|WD=Q932653|lt=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|1=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney}} lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>

'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religion|1=Religion in Japan}} of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/ |access-date=2023-08-21 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of {{ill|Japanese martial arts|en|Japanese martial arts|WD=Q1280410|lt=martial arts|1=Japanese martial arts}} such as {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}.<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}; and {{ill|Ōasahiko Shrine|en|Ōasahiko Shrine|WD=Q723417|lt=Ōasahiko Shrine|1=Ōasahiko Shrine}} in {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers.<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on [[Isuzu River|Isuzu river]] at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[:en:Ueshiba Morihei|Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life: the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[:en:Aikidō|Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself ("and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself"){{cn|date=May 2023}}
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[:en:Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist {{ill|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|en|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|WD=Q932653|lt=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|1=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney}} lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu|1=Tengu}} ** {{ill|Garuda|en|Garuda|WD=Q188676|lt=Garuda|1=Garuda}} * [[Shirahige Shrine|Shirahige Jinja]] * {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}} * [[Sarutahiko Shrine]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[co:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[co:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|WD=Q10940674|1=Ame no Wakahiko}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696|1=Honoakari}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395|1=Hosuseri}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|WD=Q10949054|1=Nigihayahi}}}} {{Short description|Deity in Shinto; leader of the earthly kami}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}}
'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religion|1=Religion in Japan}} of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/ |access-date=2023-08-21 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of {{ill|Japanese martial arts|en|Japanese martial arts|WD=Q1280410|lt=martial arts|1=Japanese martial arts}} such as {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}.<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}; and {{ill|Ōasahiko Shrine|en|Ōasahiko Shrine|WD=Q723417|lt=Ōasahiko Shrine|1=Ōasahiko Shrine}} in {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers.<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on [[Isuzu River|Isuzu river]] at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[:en:Ueshiba Morihei|Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life: the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[:en:Aikidō|Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself ("and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself"){{cn|date=May 2023}}
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[:en:Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist {{ill|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|en|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|WD=Q932653|lt=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|1=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney}} lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[co:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[co:commons:Category:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sarutahiko]][[en:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[fa:ساروتاهیکو اوکامی]][[fr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[it:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ja:サルタヒコ]][[ko:사루타히코 오카미]][[mni:ꯁꯔꯨꯇꯍꯤꯀꯣ ꯑꯣꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Sarutahiko]][[ru:Сарутахико]][[simple:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[ta:சருதாகிகோ அகாமி]][[tr:Sarutahiko Ōkami]][[zh:猿田彥]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|WD=Q10940674|1=Ame no Wakahiko}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696|1=Honoakari}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395|1=Hosuseri}}; {{ill|Nigihayahi|WD=Q10949054|1=Nigihayahi}}}} {{Short description|Deity in Shinto; leader of the earthly kami}} {{For|the asteroid|10768 Sarutahiko}}
'''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religion|1=Religion in Japan}} of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]]''. [[Norito]] also mentions him with the title {{nihongo3|great bright god, or greatly virtuous god|大明神|Daimyōjin}} instead of {{nihongo3|great god|大神|Ōkami}}. Sarutahiko Ōkami was the head of the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref> and in the [[Jinnō Shōtōki]] is said to have been the ancestor of [[Ōta-no-Mikoto|Otanomikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Otanomikoto • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/ |access-date=2023-08-21 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
Sarutahiko Ōkami is seen as a symbol of [[Misogi]], strength and guidance, which is why he is the patron of {{ill|Japanese martial arts|en|Japanese martial arts|WD=Q1280410|lt=martial arts|1=Japanese martial arts}} such as {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}.<ref>{{cite book|last=Gleason|first=William|title=The Spiritual Foundations of Aikido|year=1995|publisher=Destiny Books|isbn=0-89281-508-6|pages=18|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=S4JRDf6C4iMC&pg=PA18}}</ref> He is enshrined at {{ill|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|en|Tsubaki Grand Shrine|WD=Q3541617|lt=Tsubaki Grand Shrine|1=Tsubaki Grand Shrine}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}; first among the 2000 shrines of Sarutahiko Ōkami, Sarutahiko Jinja in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}; and {{ill|Ōasahiko Shrine|en|Ōasahiko Shrine|WD=Q723417|lt=Ōasahiko Shrine|1=Ōasahiko Shrine}} in {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and [[Sarutahiko Shrine]] in Mie Prefecture. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}'' (猿女の君) clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers.<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province. According to Kojiki, he went in Ise where a giant clam trapped his hand on [[Isuzu River|Isuzu river]] at Azaka, thus he drowned. But strangely, Sarutahiko was considered by [[:en:Ueshiba Morihei|Ueshiba Morihei]] as a kind of god of the cosmic life: the god of Aiki. According to O-Senseï, the practice of [[:en:Aikidō|Aikidō]] was practice of [[Misogi]] purification itself ("and thus, like Sarutahiko standing of ''Ame-no-Ukibashi'', standing between Heaven and Earth, being one with the Universe, and so achieving peace with the world, the next step being World Peace itself"){{cn|date=May 2023}}
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==Etymology== Sarutahiko's name consists of an etymologically obscure element, ''Saruta'', which is traditionally transcribed with {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} that suggest the meaning "monkey-field" as a sort of double entendre, followed by the [[:en:Classical Japanese language|Classical Japanese]] noun ''hiko'' "a male child of noble blood, a prince."<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=1-57607-467-6|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/70 70]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration}}</ref> Thus, Sarutahiko Ōkami's embellished name could be roughly translated into English as "Great God, Prince Saruta." Many variant pronunciations of his name exist, including Sarudabiko and Sadahiko. Although it is usually not written, the Japanese genitive case marker, ''-no'', is often suffixed to his name in speech when it is followed immediately by one of his honorific titles, such as ''Ōkami'' or ''Mikoto''.{{Citation needed|date=August 2012}}
Anthropologist {{ill|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|en|Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|WD=Q932653|lt=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney|1=Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney}} lists three factors that identify Sarutahiko as a [[Monkeys in Japanese culture#Religion|monkey deity]]: ''saru'' means "monkey", his features "include red buttocks, which are a prominent characteristic of Japanese macaques", and as macaques gather shellfish at low tide, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' says his hand got caught in a shell while fishing and "a monkey with one hand caught in a shell is a frequent theme of Japanese folktales".<ref>Ohnuki-Tierney, Emiko (1987), ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual'', Princeton University Press, pp. 42-43.</ref>
==Title== {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|en|lt=Izanami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|lt=Sashikuni|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|lt=Inari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
==References in music== The name of this deity appears incorrectly spelled as "Sarundasico" in [[:en:Puccini|Puccini]]'s opera ''[[Madama Butterfly]]''.<ref>{{cite web|title=2004 Regular Opera Performance "Madama Butterfly / revised edition"|url=http://www.minna-no-opera.com/eng/opera/2004_btt_gui_e.htm|publisher=NPO Opera del Popolo|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref>
